item.title: TechCrunch Mobility: A stunning lack of transparency
item.description: Senator Markey sent letters to seven autonomous vehicle companies—including Aurora, May Mobility, Motional, Nuro, Tesla, Waymo, and Zoox—demanding answers about their use of remote assistance operators. He described the companies' silence as a "stunning lack of transparency" and urged the NHTSA to investigate. Tesla admitted its remote workers can temporarily assume direct vehicle control in emergencies, a practice that starkly differs from mere assistance. Markey is also pushing legislation to impose stricter guardrails on the deployment of remote operators in self‑driving cars.

item.title: Polymarket took down wagers tied to rescue of downed Air Force officer
item.description: Prediction market platform Polymarket removed betting markets linked to the rescue timeline of a downed Air Force officer after criticism from a Democratic congressman. The decision follows broader scrutiny of wagering sites for encouraging risky financial behavior. Polymarket is now offering free groceries to New Yorkers in an effort to improve its public image, while regulators continue to examine the legal implications of real‑world event betting.

item.title: Can orbital data centers help justify a massive valuation for SpaceX?
item.description: SpaceX has reportedly filed confidential paperwork for a $75 billion IPO that could value the company at $1.75 trillion. A major focus of the filing is the company's push for orbital data centers, aiming to alleviate public opposition to terrestrial facilities. Startup Starcloud recently raised $170 million to build space‑based data hubs, positioning itself as a potential unicorn. Analysts argue that while the engineering challenges are significant, the social acceptance hurdle on Earth may prove even tougher.

item.title: Embattled startup Delve has 'parted ways' with Y Combinator
item.description: YC‑backed startup Delve, whose reputation has been marred by allegations of fake compliance documents and open‑source plagiarism, announced it is no longer affiliated with Y Combinator. Former employees and whistleblowers claimed the company passed off community‑built tools as proprietary and falsified audit reports. The separation follows a series of public leaks that damaged investor confidence and raised questions about governance practices in early‑stage tech firms.

item.title: Sony's PS5 Price Hikes Prove This Console Generation Is Far From Over
item.description: Sony announced price increases across several PS5 bundles, citing rising component costs and a strategic shift toward premium accessories. The moves signal that the current console generation remains profitable despite supply chain pressures. Analysts note that Sony is likely leveraging its strong market position to introduce higher‑margin peripherals, while competitors such as Microsoft and Nintendo are preparing next‑gen hardware to maintain consumer interest.

item.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
item.description: A wave of cyber‑crime groups have begun distributing the recent Claude AI model code leak bundled with new malware designed to exfiltrate data from vulnerable systems. The leak, originally sourced from an insider at Anthropic, has spurred a surge in credential‑stealing tools that target developers who download the code for research. Security firms warn that the combined threat could accelerate the weaponization of advanced language models in ransomware campaigns.

item.title: Iran threatens OpenAI's Stargate data center in Abu Dhabi
item.description: Iranian officials issued a public warning that they could target OpenAI's new "Stargate" data center in Abu Dhabi, citing concerns over AI-generated disinformation. The statement coincides with escalating geopolitical tensions and marks the first time a nation has explicitly threatened an AI infrastructure facility. OpenAI responded by reinforcing its physical security protocols and affirming that the center complies with all international data‑protective standards.

item.title: Apple isn't giving up on its App Store fight with Epic just yet
item.description: Apple filed a petition with the U.S. Supreme Court seeking review of a December 2025 ruling that found the company in contempt for imposing a 27 percent fee on external in‑app payments. Simultaneously, Apple asked the Ninth Circuit to pause the same ruling while it prepares an appeal. The move underscores Apple's determination to maintain its App Store economics amid ongoing antitrust scrutiny and a high‑profile battle with Epic Games.

item.title: Artemis II is more than halfway to the Moon
item.description: NASA's Artemis II mission, the first crewed flight of the Orion spacecraft, passed the halfway point on its lunar trajectory on April 6. The crew will perform a free‑return loop around the Moon before splashing down later in the week. The mission serves as a critical test of deep‑space life‑support systems and paves the way for the planned Artemis III landing on the lunar surface later this year.

item.title: A Single Strike Won't Shut Off the Gulf's Desalination System
item.description: Engineers behind the Gulf region's massive desalination network disclosed that the system is built with multiple redundant layers, making it resilient to isolated sabotage attempts. While cyber‑physical attacks remain a concern, the infrastructure's design includes automated fail‑over mechanisms and decentralized control nodes that prevent a single breach from halting water production. Officials stress ongoing investments in cybersecurity to protect the critical supply chain.

item.title: Samsung’s 2 New Midrange Phones Get Price Hikes and Small Updates
item.description: Samsung raised the prices of its Galaxy A37 and A57 midrange smartphones after a modest hardware refresh that added a faster processor and improved battery life. The companies cited rising component costs and a stronger dollar as reasons for the increase. Analysts predict the price bump could narrow the gap between Samsung's midrange line and competing offerings from Xiaomi and Oppo, potentially reshaping the budget market segment.
